title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Ben Morris, who makes bags from recycled materials.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Ben Morris, who started a small company making bags from recycled materials. Ben, where did the idea come from? || Guest: I was travelling by train and noticed how many advertising banners were being taken down after a sports event. They were huge and colourful, but they were going to be thrown away. [20]I wondered if the strong material could be cut and sewn into bags instead[/20]. I made one for myself first, and people kept asking where I had bought it. || Interviewer: Was it easy to start selling them? || Guest: No. I knew the bags were interesting, but I didn't know much about business. A friend helped me build a website, and my sister took good photos. [21]The real problem was finding people who could sew the thick material neatly[/21]. Ordinary machines broke, so we had to use a small workshop that usually repaired boat covers. || Interviewer: What do customers like about the bags? || Guest: They like the environmental idea, of course, but they also enjoy the fact that every bag looks different. [22]Some customers say they like having a bag with part of a picture or colour that no one else has[/22]. We can't make two exactly the same, even if we try. || Interviewer: Do you ever run out of materials? || Guest: Sometimes. We collect banners from festivals, shops and exhibitions, but they arrive at different times of year. [23]Winter is the most difficult season because there are fewer outdoor events[/23]. In summer, we usually have too much material and not enough space to store it. || Interviewer: Has running the company changed the way you shop? || Guest: Definitely. I used to buy things quickly if they were cheap. Now I ask where something comes from and how long it will last. [24]I don't buy less because I feel guilty; I buy less because I prefer things that I can use for years[/24]. That feels more positive. || Interviewer: What would you like the company to do next? || Guest: We are testing some laptop cases, but the design is not quite right yet. [25]Our next product will probably be a simple school bag because parents keep asking for one[/25]. It must be strong, light and easy to clean, so it will take time to get right. || Interviewer: Do people understand why the bags cost more than some ordinary bags? || Guest: Usually, once we explain the process. We have to collect the material, wash it, cut around damaged parts and sew every bag by hand. It takes longer than using new fabric from a roll. I try to be honest about that, because customers appreciate knowing where their money goes. || Interviewer: What have you learnt from running a small company? || Guest: I have learnt to ask for help earlier. At first I tried to do everything myself, from emails to packing orders. That made me tired and mistakes happened. Now I work with a small team, and the business feels healthier. || Interviewer: Do you have a favourite bag from the ones you've made? || Guest: I kept the first one, although it is not as neat as the bags we sell now. It reminds me that an idea does not have to be perfect at the beginning. You can improve it slowly by listening to people and solving one problem at a time.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Ben Morris, who started a small company making bags from recycled materials. Ben, where did the idea come from?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I was travelling by train and noticed how many advertising banners were being taken down after a sports event. They were huge and colourful, but they were going to be thrown away. I wondered if the strong material could be cut and sewn into bags instead. I made one for myself first, and people kept asking where I had bought it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Was it easy to start selling them?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: No. I knew the bags were interesting, but I didn't know much about business. A friend helped me build a website, and my sister took good photos. The real problem was finding people who could sew the thick material neatly. Ordinary machines broke, so we had to use a small workshop that usually repaired boat covers.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do customers like about the bags?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They like the environmental idea, of course, but they also enjoy the fact that every bag looks different. Some customers say they like having a bag with part of a picture or colour that no one else has. We can't make two exactly the same, even if we try.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you ever run out of materials?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Sometimes. We collect banners from festivals, shops and exhibitions, but they arrive at different times of year. Winter is the most difficult season because there are fewer outdoor events. In summer, we usually have too much material and not enough space to store it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has running the company changed the way you shop?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Definitely. I used to buy things quickly if they were cheap. Now I ask where something comes from and how long it will last. I don't buy less because I feel guilty; I buy less because I prefer things that I can use for years. That feels more positive.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you like the company to do next?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We are testing some laptop cases, but the design is not quite right yet. Our next product will probably be a simple school bag because parents keep asking for one. It must be strong, light and easy to clean, so it will take time to get right.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do people understand why the bags cost more than some ordinary bags?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Usually, once we explain the process. We have to collect the material, wash it, cut around damaged parts and sew every bag by hand. It takes longer than using new fabric from a roll. I try to be honest about that, because customers appreciate knowing where their money goes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What have you learnt from running a small company?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I have learnt to ask for help earlier. At first I tried to do everything myself, from emails to packing orders. That made me tired and mistakes happened. Now I work with a small team, and the business feels healthier.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you have a favourite bag from the ones you've made?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I kept the first one, although it is not as neat as the bags we sell now. It reminds me that an idea does not have to be perfect at the beginning. You can improve it slowly by listening to people and solving one problem at a time.

questions.question: What gave Ben the idea for his bags?

options.A: Seeing useful material that was going to be wasted.
options.B: Buying a colourful bag while he was travelling.
options.C: Watching people make bags at a sports event.

questions.explanation: Ben says he saw advertising banners being thrown away and wondered if the material could become bags.

questions.question: What was the main difficulty when Ben started?

options.A: Making a website for the business.
options.B: Taking attractive photographs of the products.
options.C: Finding a workshop that could sew the material.

questions.explanation: Ben says the real problem was finding people who could sew the thick material neatly.

questions.question: What do some customers especially like?

options.C: The bags are easy to repair.

questions.explanation: Ben says customers like having a bag with a picture or colour that no one else has.

questions.question: Why is winter difficult for Ben's company?

options.B: There are fewer events providing material.
options.C: The workshop has less storage space.

questions.explanation: Ben says winter is difficult because there are fewer outdoor events.

questions.question: How has Ben's shopping changed?

options.A: He only buys recycled products now.
options.B: He buys cheap things less often.
options.C: He chooses things that will last a long time.

questions.explanation: Ben says he buys less because he prefers things he can use for years.

questions.question: What product is Ben likely to make next?
questions.explanation: Ben says the next product will probably be a simple school bag.
